segments.text: Watch the feint here, because this is the exact moment the southpaw gets the champion to reach.
The jab freezes him, the level change pulls the guard down, and the left hook lands clean on the exit.
He is already hurt, but the scary part is how the follow-up body shot takes away the reset.
That is why the referee steps in fast, because the counter lands before the champion can recover his feet.
If you are breaking down this knockout, the key is not the last punch, it is the first fake that wins the angle.
Now look at round three again, because the same setup appears twice before the finish and nobody notices it in real time.
This is the mistake fighters make against pressure strikers, they back straight up and give away the cage line.
Once the fence is behind him, the takedown threat disappears and the boxer can throw without respecting the level change.
That is why the corner is screaming at him to circle off, but he stays planted and the counter right hand clips him again.
The crowd only remembers the knockout, but coaches will tell you the distance management wins this whole sequence.
Even in a title fight, one bad step and one lazy guard return is all it takes for the momentum to flip.
So if you want the short to hit, start on the fake, show the clean connection, and end on the referee wave-off.
